Figure 106 ldmatrix fragment layout for one 8x16 matrix with 8-bit elements containing 4-bit/6-bit data

Optional qualifier `.trans` indicates that the matrix is loaded in column-major format. However,
for 16x16 matrices, `.trans` is mandatory.

The `ldmatrix` instruction is treated as a weak memory operation in the [Memory Consistency Model](#memory-consistency-model).

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 6.5.

Support for `::cta` sub-qualifier introduced in PTX ISA version 7.8.

Support for `.m16n16`, `.m8n16` shapes introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Support for `.b8` type with `ldmatrix` is introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Support for `.src_fmt`, `.dst_fmt` qualifiers introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Requires `sm_75` or higher.

Shapes `.m16n16`, `.m8n16` are supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_120a`
* And are supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  > + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  > + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  > + `sm_120f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Type `.b8` with `ldmatrix` is supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_120a`
* And are supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  > + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  > + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  > + `sm_120f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Qualifiers `.src_fmt`, `.dst_fmt` are supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_120a`
* And are supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  > + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  > + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  > + `sm_120f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
// Load a single 8x8 matrix using 64-bit addressing
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x1.shared::cta.b16 {d}, [addr];

// Load two 8x8 matrices in column-major format
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d<2>;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x2.trans.shared.b16 {d0, d1}, [addr];

// Load four 8x8 matrices
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d<4>;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x4.b16 {d0, d1, d2, d3}, [addr];

// Load one 16x16 matrices of 64-bit elements and transpose them
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d<2>;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n16.x1.trans.shared.b8 {d0, d1}, [addr];

// Load two 16x16 matrices of 64-bit elements and transpose them
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d<4>;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n16.x2.trans.shared::cta.b8 {d0, d1, d2, d3}, [addr];

// Load two 16x16 matrices of 6-bit elements and transpose them
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 d<4>;
ldmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n16.x2.trans.shared::cta.b8x16.b6x16_p32 {d0, d1, d2, d3}, [addr];
```

##### 9.7.14.5.16. [Warp-level matrix store instruction: `stmatrix`](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-stmatrix)

`stmatrix`

Collectively store one or more matrices to shared memory.

Syntax

```
stmatrix.sync.aligned.shape.num{.trans}{.ss}.type [p], r;

.shape  = {.m8n8, .m16n8};
.num    = {.x1, .x2, .x4};
.ss     = {.shared{::cta}};
.type   = {.b16, .b8};
```

Description

Collectively store one or more matrices across all threads in a warp to the location indicated by
the address operand `p`, in `.shared` state space. If no state space is provided, generic
addressing is used, such that the address in `p` points into `.shared` space. If the generic
address doesn’t fall in `.shared` state space, then the behavior is undefined.

The `.shape` qualifier indicates the dimensions of the matrices being loaded. Each matrix element
holds 16-bit or 8-bit data as indicated by the `.type` qualifier.

`.m16n8` shape is valid only for `.b8` type.

The values `.x1`, `.x2` and `.x4` for `.num` indicate one, two or four matrices
respectively.

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `stmatrix` causes the executing thread to wait
until all threads in the warp execute the same `stmatrix` instruction before resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warp must execute the same
`stmatrix` instruction. In conditionally executed code, an `stmatrix` instruction should only be
used if it is known that all threads in the warp evaluate the condition identically, otherwise the
behavior is undefined.

The behavior of `stmatrix` is undefined if all threads do not use the same qualifiers, or if any
thread in the warp has exited.

The source operand `r` is a brace-enclosed vector expression consisting of 1, 2, or 4 32-bit
registers as per the value of `.num`. Each component of the vector expression holds a fragment
from the corresponding matrix.

Supported addressing modes for `p` are described in [Addresses as Operands](#addresses-as-operands).

Consecutive instances of row need not be stored contiguously in memory. The eight addresses required
for each matrix are provided by eight threads, depending upon the value of `.num` as shown in the
following table. Each address corresponds to the start of a matrix row. Addresses addr0–addr7
correspond to the rows of the first matrix, addresses addr8–addr15 correspond to the rows of the
second matrix, and so on.

| `.num` | Threads 0–7 | Threads 8–15 | Threads 16–23 | Threads 24–31 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `.x1` | addr0–addr7 | – | – | – |
| `.x2` | addr0–addr7 | addr8–addr15 | – | – |
| `.x4` | addr0–addr7 | addr8–addr15 | addr16–addr23 | addr24–addr31 |

When storing 8x8 matrices, a group of four consecutive threads stores 16 bytes. The matrix addresses
must be naturally aligned accordingly.

Each thread in a warp stores fragments of a row, with thread 0 storing the first fragment from its
register `r`, and so on. A group of four threads stores an entire row of the matrix as shown in
[Figure 107](#mma-stmatrix-fragments).

STMATRIX FRAGMENT LAYOUT: ONE 8x8 MATRIX WITH 16-BIT ELEMENTS

1. MATRIX DIMENSIONS AND TYPE
Matrix size: 8 rows x 8 columns
Element type: 16-bit elements (e.g., .f16 or .bf16)
This describes the stmatrix instruction's fragment layout for a single 8x8 tile.

2. GRID STRUCTURE
Rows: 8 matrix rows (labeled 0 through 7)
Columns: 8 matrix columns (labeled 0 through 7)
Total threads involved: T0 through T31 (32 threads)
Each cell in the table is labeled as "Tx:r" where x is the thread index and r is the register holding that element.

3. COMPLETE ELEMENT-TO-THREAD-REGISTER MAPPING (Row by Row)

Row 0: Col0=T0:r, Col1=T0:r, Col2=T1:r, Col3=T1:r, Col4=T2:r, Col5=T2:r, Col6=T3:r, Col7=T3:r

Row 2: Col0=T8:r, Col1=T8:r, Col2=T9:r, Col3=T9:r, Col4=T10:r, Col5=T10:r, Col6=T11:r, Col7=T11:r
Row 3: Col0=T12:r, Col1=T12:r, Col2=T13:r, Col3=T13:r, Col4=T14:r, Col5=T14:r, Col6=T15:r, Col7=T15:r
Row 4: Col0=T16:r, Col1=T16:r, Col2=T17:r, Col3=T17:r, Col4=T18:r, Col5=T18:r, Col6=T19:r, Col7=T19:r

4. DATA TYPE
16-bit elements per cell. Each register r holds two 16-bit values packed into one 32-bit register (since two consecutive columns in the same row map to the same thread and same register, e.g., Row0 Col0 and Col1 are both T0:r).

5. GROUPING PATTERNS
Each matrix row is covered by exactly 4 threads.
Row 0: T0, T1, T2, T3
Row 1: T4, T5, T6, T7
Row 2: T8, T9, T10, T11
Row 3: T12, T13, T14, T15
Row 4: T16, T17, T18, T19
Row 5: T20, T21, T22, T23
Row 6: T24, T25, T26, T27
Row 7: T28, T29, T30, T31

Each thread covers exactly 2 consecutive columns within its assigned row. Specifically:
Within each row, the 4 threads cover column pairs: T(base+0) covers cols 0-1, T(base+1) covers cols 2-3, T(base+2) covers cols 4-5, T(base+3) covers cols 6-7.

6. PACKING WITHIN REGISTERS
Each thread holds exactly one 32-bit register r for this 8x8 matrix.
That single register r contains two 16-bit elements packed together (the two elements from the two consecutive columns assigned to that thread in its assigned row).
For example, T0 holds register r containing the two 16-bit values at (Row0, Col0) and (Row0, Col1) packed into one 32-bit register.
This applies uniformly to all 32 threads T0 through T31.

Figure 107 stmatrix fragment layout for one 8x8 matrix with 16-bit elements

When `.num` = `.x2`, the elements of the second matrix are storedd from the next source register
in each thread as per the layout in above table. Similarly, when `.num` = `.x4`, elements of the
third and fourth matrices are stored from the subsequent source registers in each thread.

For 16x8 matrix shape, each of the 32 threads in the warp provides four elements of data per matrix.

Each element in the source operand `r` is of type `.b32` and contains four 8 bit elements `e0`,
`e1`, `e2`, `e3` with `e0` and `e3` containing the LSB and MSB respectively of register `r`.





[Figure: mma-stmatrix-fragments-168]
STMATRIX FRAGMENT LAYOUT: 16x8 MATRIX WITH 8-BIT ELEMENTS

=== MATRIX DIMENSIONS AND TYPE ===
Matrix size: 16 rows x 8 columns
Element type: 8-bit elements (integer, e.g., .u8 or .s8)
Total elements: 128
This is used with stmatrix instruction for storing matrix fragments to shared memory.

=== GRID STRUCTURE ===
Rows: 16 matrix rows (0 through 15)
Columns: 8 matrix columns (0 through 7)
Threads involved: T0 through T31 (32 threads total)
Registers per thread used: 2 registers (each register holds 4 bytes = four 8-bit elements, elements e0/e1 in first register, e2/e3 in second register)

=== ELEMENT PACKING WITHIN REGISTERS ===
Each 32-bit register holds 4 eight-bit elements packed together.
Register 0 (r0): holds elements e0, e1, e2, e3 — but the layout splits them:
  e0 and e1 appear in matrix rows 0-7 (upper half)
  e2 and e3 appear in matrix rows 8-15 (lower half)
So effectively each thread contributes 2 elements to the upper 8 rows and 2 elements to the lower 8 rows, using what appears to be 2 registers total (one register covering e0+e1, another covering e2+e3), or alternatively 4 elements packed in a single register with e0,e1,e2,e3 as byte lanes.

Figure 108 stmatrix fragment layout for one 16x8 matrix with 8 bit elements

Optional qualifier `.trans` indicates that the matrix is stored in column-major format. However,
for 16x8 matrices, `.trans` is mandatory.

The `stmatrix` instruction is treated as a weak memory operation in the [Memory Consistency Model](#memory-consistency-model).

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 7.8.

Support for `.m16n8` shape is introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Support for `.b8` type with `stmatrix` is introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Requires `sm_90` or higher.

Shape `.m16n8` is supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_120a`
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  > + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  > + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  > + `sm_120f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Type `.b8` with `stmatrix` is supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_120a`
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  > + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  > + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  > + `sm_120f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
// Store a single 8x8 matrix using 64-bit addressing
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x1.shared.b16 [addr], {r};

// Store two 8x8 matrices in column-major format
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r<2>;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x2.trans.shared::cta.b16 [addr], {r0, r1};

// Store four 8x8 matrices
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r<4>;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m8n8.x4.b16 [addr], {r0, r1, r2, r3};

// Store a single 16x8 matrix using generic addressing
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n8.x1.trans.shared.b8 [addr], {r};

// Store two 16x8 matrices
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r<2>;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n8.x2.trans.shared::cta.b8 [addr],{r0, r1};

// Store four 16x8 matrices
.reg .b64 addr;
.reg .b32 r<4>;
stmatrix.sync.aligned.m16n8.x4.b8 [addr], {r0, r1, r2, r3};
```

##### 9.7.14.5.17. [Warp-level matrix transpose instruction: `movmatrix`](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-movmatrix)

`movmatrix`

Transpose a matrix in registers across the warp.

Syntax

```
movmatrix.sync.aligned.shape.trans.type d, a;

.shape  = {.m8n8};
.type   = {.b16};
```

Description

Move a row-major matrix across all threads in a warp, reading elements from source `a`, and
writing the transposed elements to destination `d`.

The `.shape` qualifier indicates the dimensions of the matrix being transposed. Each matrix
element holds 16-bit data as indicated by the `.type` qualifier.

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `movmatrix` causes the executing thread to wait
until all threads in the warp execute the same `movmatrix` instruction before resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warp must execute the same
`movmatrix` instruction. In conditionally executed code, a `movmatrix` instruction should only
be used if it is known that all threads in the warp evaluate the condition identically, otherwise
the behavior is undefined.

Operands `a` and `d` are 32-bit registers containing fragments of the input matrix and the
resulting matrix respectively. The mandatory qualifier `.trans` indicates that the resulting
matrix in `d` is a transpose of the input matrix specified by `a`.

Each thread in a warp holds a fragment of a row of the input matrix, with thread 0 holding the first
fragment in register `a`, and so on. A group of four threads holds an entire row of the input
matrix as shown in [Figure 109](#mma-movmatrix-fragments-src).

  row2, row3: %laneid = 29, dst=d
  row4, row5: %laneid = 30, dst=d
  row6, row7: %laneid = 31, dst=d

LANE-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING (complete list, laneid -> matrix position):
laneid  0: col0, row0-row1, dst=d
laneid  1: col0, row2-row3, dst=d
laneid  2: col0, row4-row5, dst=d
laneid  3: col0, row6-row7, dst=d
laneid  4: col1, row0-row1, dst=d
laneid  5: col1, row2-row3, dst=d
laneid  6: col1, row4-row5, dst=d
laneid  7: col1, row6-row7, dst=d
laneid  8: col2, row0-row1, dst=d
laneid  9: col2, row2-row3, dst=d
laneid 10: col2, row4-row5, dst=d
laneid 11: col2, row6-row7, dst=d
laneid 12: col3, row0-row1, dst=d
laneid 13: col3, row2-row3, dst=d
laneid 14: col3, row4-row5, dst=d
laneid 15: col3, row6-row7, dst=d
laneid 16: col4, row0-row1, dst=d
laneid 17: col4, row2-row3, dst=d
laneid 18: col4, row4-row5, dst=d
laneid 19: col4, row6-row7, dst=d

GROUPING PATTERN:
- Each column (col0-col7) is handled by 4 consecutive lanes.
- col0: lanes 0-3; col1: lanes 4-7; col2: lanes 8-11; col3: lanes 12-15
- col4: lanes 16-19; col5: lanes 20-23; col6: lanes 24-27; col7: lanes 28-31
- Within each column group of 4 lanes, each lane covers a pair of rows (row0-1, row2-3, row4-5, row6-7).
- Each lane holds one element in register dst=d (single register, no packing noted explicitly in figure).

DATA TYPE: Not explicitly specified in this figure. The register is labeled dst=d with no type annotation shown.

REGISTER PACKING: Each lane holds one value in a single register named d (dst=d). No multi-element packing is indicated in this figure.

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 7.8.

Target ISA Notes

Requires `sm_75` or higher.

Examples

```
.reg .b32 d, a;

```

#### 9.7.14.6. [Matrix multiply-accumulate operation using `mma.sp` instruction with sparse matrix A](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-for-sparse-mma)

This section describes warp-level `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` instruction with sparse matrix A.
This variant of the `mma` operation can be used when A is a structured sparse matrix with 50%
zeros in each row distributed in a shape-specific granularity. For an `MxNxK` sparse
`mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` operation, the `MxK` matrix A is packed into `MxK/2` elements.
For each K-wide row of matrix A, 50% elements are zeros and the remaining K/2 non-zero elements
are packed in the operand representing matrix A. The mapping of these K/2 elements to the
corresponding K-wide row is provided explicitly as metadata.

##### 9.7.14.6.1. [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage)

Granularity of sparse matrix A is defined as the ratio of the number of non-zero elements in a
sub-chunk of the matrix row to the total number of elements in that sub-chunk where the size of the
sub-chunk is shape-specific. For example, in a `16x16` matrix A, sparsity is expected to be at 2:4
granularity, i.e. each 4-element vector (i.e. a sub-chunk of 4 consecutive elements) of a matrix row
contains 2 zeros. Index of each non-zero element in a sub-chunk is stored in the metadata
operand. Values `0b0000`, `0b0101`, `0b1010`, `0b1111` are invalid values for metadata and
will result in undefined behavior. In a group of four consecutive threads, one or more threads store
the metadata for the whole group depending upon the matrix shape. These threads are specified using
an additional *sparsity selector* operand.

[Figure 111](#sparse-mma-storage-example) shows an example of a 16x16 matrix A represented in sparse format and sparsity
selector indicating which thread in a group of four consecutive threads stores the metadata.

Granularities for different matrix shapes and data types are described below.

Sparse `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` with half-precision and `.bf16` type

For the `.m16n8k16` and `.m16n8k32` `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` operations, matrix A is
structured sparse at a granularity of 2:4. In other words, each chunk of four adjacent elements
in a row of matrix A has two zeros and two non-zero elements. Only the two non-zero elements are
stored in the operand representing matrix A and their positions in the four-wide chunk in matrix
A are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata operand. For `mma.sp::ordered_metadata`,
`0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110` are the meaningful values
of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: f16-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .f16/.bf16 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the relationship between a four-wide chunk from a row in matrix A, the indices of elements within that chunk, and how the sparse matrix operand and metadata are derived from the 2:4 structured sparsity pattern.

SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
This is a 64-bit wide register showing 4 elements (each 16 bits wide for f16/bf16):
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: y  (non-zero element)
  Bits 47-32: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (non-zero element)

The pattern is: y, 0, 0, x — meaning elements at positions 3 and 0 are non-zero; elements at positions 2 and 1 are zero. This is a valid 2:4 sparsity pattern (2 non-zeros out of every 4 elements).

SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
This is a 64-bit wide field showing the 2-bit index of each of the 4 elements:
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: 11  (index 3, corresponding to element y)
  Bits 47-32: 10  (index 2, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 01  (index 1, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  00  (index 0, corresponding to element x)

Each element in the four-wide chunk is assigned a 2-bit index: 00=position 0, 01=position 1, 10=position 2, 11=position 3.

SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
This is a 32-bit wide register holding only the 2 non-zero elements (compressed):
Bit positions: 31 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 31-16: y  (the non-zero element from position 3)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (the non-zero element from position 0)

The sparse operand contains only the non-zero values packed together, discarding the zeros.

SECTION 4: METADATA
This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the positions of the two non-zero elements:
Bit positions: 3 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index of the high non-zero element, y, which is at position 3)
  Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index of the low non-zero element, x, which is at position 0)

METADATA ENCODING RULES:
- Each pair of non-zero elements selected from a group of 4 requires 4 bits of metadata total.
- The lower 2 bits (bits 1-0) encode the index (0–3) of the first (lower) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- The upper 2 bits (bits 3-2) encode the index (0–3) of the second (higher) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- In this example: lower non-zero x is at index 00 (position 0), upper non-zero y is at index 11 (position 3).
- Metadata value = 0b1100 = 0xC (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00).

RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY:
Original 4-element chunk: [y, 0, 0, x] at indices [3, 2, 1, 0]
Non-zero elements selected: x (index 0), y (index 3)
Sparse operand (32-bit): [y | x] — y in upper 16 bits, x in lower 16 bits
Metadata (4-bit): [11 | 00] — upper 2 bits = index of y (3), lower 2 bits = index of x (0)

Figure 112 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k16`: One thread within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the metadata for
  the entire group. This thread is indicated by a value in {0, 1, 2, 3}.
* `m16n8k32`: A thread-pair within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the sparsity
  metadata. Hence, the sparsity selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or 1 (threads T2, T3);
  any other value results in an undefined behavior.

Sparse `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` with `.tf32` type

When matrix A has `.tf32` elements, matrix A is structured sparse at a granularity of 1:2. In
other words, each chunk of two adjacent elements in a row of matrix A has one zero and one non-zero
element. Only the non-zero elements are stored in the operand for matrix A and their positions in a
two-wide chunk in matrix A are indicated by the 4-bit index in the metadata. `0b1110` and
`0b0100` are the only meaningful index values; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: tf32-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .tf32 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how a two-wide chunk from matrix A is compressed into a sparse matrix operand, and how the metadata encodes which elements are selected. The data type is .tf32.

============================================================
SECTION 1: TWO-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide register (bits 63 down to 0), split into two 32-bit halves:

Bit positions:  63 .............. 32 | 31 .............. 0
Contents:              x             |         0

- Bits [63:32]: labeled "x" (non-zero value, kept)
- Bits [31:0]:  labeled "0" (zero value, pruned/discarded)

This represents a 2:4 structured sparsity pattern where 1 out of 2 elements is non-zero (for tf32, the chunk is 2-wide, meaning 2 elements per group, and 1 is selected).

============================================================
SECTION 2: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 32-bit wide register (bits 31 down to 0):

Bit positions:  31 .............. 0
Contents:              x

- Bits [31:0]: labeled "x" (the single non-zero element retained from the two-wide chunk)

The sparse operand is half the width of the original chunk because only the non-zero elements are stored.

============================================================
SECTION 3: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE TWO-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide field (bits 63 down to 0), divided into four 16-bit segments, each holding a 2-bit index value:

Bit positions:  63 .... 48 | 47 .... 32 | 31 .... 16 | 15 .... 0
Index values:      11      |     10      |     01      |    00

- Bits [63:48]: index value = 11 (binary)
- Bits [47:32]: index value = 10 (binary)
- Bits [31:16]: index value = 01 (binary)
- Bits [15:0]:  index value = 00 (binary)

These are the 2-bit indices indicating which element within each two-wide chunk is the non-zero element. Each 2-bit field encodes the column position (0 or 1) of the selected non-zero element within its respective 2-element group.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0), divided into two 2-bit segments:

Bit positions:  3 .... 2 | 1 .... 0
Values:            11    |    10

- Bits [3:2]: metadata value = 11 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the upper (second) group
- Bits [1:0]: metadata value = 10 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the lower (first) group

The metadata register is 4 bits total, encoding two 2-bit selector fields.

============================================================
ENCODING RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY
============================================================

For .tf32 type, each element is 32 bits wide. The two-wide chunk is 64 bits (2 x 32-bit tf32 elements). The sparse operand retains only the non-zero element (32 bits). The metadata uses 2 bits per element group to indicate which of the 2 positions in the chunk holds the non-zero value.

Metadata bit encoding per 2-bit field:
- 00 = element at position 0 (bits [31:0] of chunk) is selected
- 01 = element at position 1 (bits [63:32] of chunk) is selected
- 10 = element at position 2 (used in wider chunks; for 2-wide, encodes position)
- 11 = element at position 3 (used in wider chunks)

In this specific example:
- Lower metadata field [1:0] = 10: selects position 2 within its group
- Upper metadata field [3:2] = 11: selects position 3 within its group

The "Indices of elements within the two-wide chunk" (64-bit field with values 11, 10, 01, 00) shows the full index mapping across all groups, while the actual metadata stored is the compressed 4-bit version (bits 3:0) containing only the two relevant 2-bit selectors (11 and 10) corresponding to the non-zero element positions.

Figure 113 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.tf32` type.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k8`: One thread within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the metadata for
  the entire group. This thread is indicated by a value in {0, 1, 2, 3}.
* `m16n8k16`: A thread-pair within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the sparsity
  metadata. Hence, the sparsity selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or 1 (threads T2, T3);
  any other value results in an undefined behavior.

Sparse `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` with integer type

When matrices A and B have `.u8`/`.s8` elements, matrix A is structured sparse at a granularity
of 2:4. In other words, each chunk of four adjacent elements in a row of matrix A have two zeroes
and two non-zero elements. Only the two non-zero elements are stored in sparse matrix and their
positions in the four-wide chunk are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata. For
`mma.sp::ordered_metadata`, `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110`
are the meaningful values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: u8s8-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .u8/.s8 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how a four-wide chunk from a row in matrix A is compressed into a sparse matrix operand, and how the corresponding metadata encodes which elements were selected.

============================================================
SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 32-bit register (bits 31 down to 0) containing four 8-bit elements arranged as follows:

Bit positions: 31 ............................................. 0
Fields (left to right, high to low bits):
  [31:24] = y   (non-zero element)
  [23:16] = 0   (zero element)
  [15:8]  = 0   (zero element)
  [7:0]   = x   (non-zero element)

So the four-wide chunk contains: y, 0, 0, x
Two elements are non-zero (x and y), two elements are zero (the middle two).
This represents 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero out of every 4 elements).

============================================================
SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This shows the 2-bit index assigned to each position within the four-wide chunk:

Bit positions: 31 ............................................. 0
Fields (left to right, high to low bits):
  [31:24] position index = 11  (binary 11 = decimal 3, i.e., the 4th element = y)
  [23:16] position index = 10  (binary 10 = decimal 2, i.e., the 3rd element = 0)
  [15:8]  position index = 01  (binary 01 = decimal 1, i.e., the 2nd element = 0)
  [7:0]   position index = 00  (binary 00 = decimal 0, i.e., the 1st element = x)

Each element in the four-wide chunk is labeled with its 2-bit positional index: 00, 01, 10, 11.

============================================================
SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 16-bit compressed register (bits 15 down to 0) containing only the two non-zero elements:

Bit positions: 15 .............. 0
Fields (left to right, high to low bits):
  [15:8] = y   (the non-zero element from position 11)
  [7:0]  = x   (the non-zero element from position 00)

The two zero elements are dropped; only x and y are stored in the sparse operand.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit metadata field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the positions of the two selected non-zero elements:

Bit positions: 3 .... 1 .... 0
Fields (left to right, high to low bits):
  [3:2] = 11   (2-bit index indicating that the second non-zero element y came from position 11 in the four-wide chunk)
  [1:0] = 00   (2-bit index indicating that the first non-zero element x came from position 00 in the four-wide chunk)

METADATA ENCODING RULE:
- Each pair of metadata bits encodes the original 2-bit positional index (00, 01, 10, or 11) of one non-zero element within its four-wide chunk.
- Bits [1:0] of metadata = index of the first non-zero element (x) = 00
- Bits [3:2] of metadata = index of the second non-zero element (y) = 11
- Total metadata width per pair of non-zero elements = 4 bits

============================================================
SUMMARY OF ENCODING RELATIONSHIP
============================================================

Four-wide chunk (32-bit):  [ y | 0 | 0 | x ]   positions: [11 | 10 | 01 | 00]
Sparse operand (16-bit):   [ y | x ]             (only non-zero elements retained)
Metadata (4-bit):          [ 11 | 00 ]           (positions of y and x respectively)

The metadata value 11 in bits [3:2] tells the hardware that the second stored element (y) originally resided at position 3 (index 11) in the four-wide chunk.
The metadata value 00 in bits [1:0] tells the hardware that the first stored element (x) originally resided at position 0 (index 00) in the four-wide chunk.

This is consistent with 2:4 structured sparsity for .u8/.s8 data types, where each 4-element group contributes exactly 2 non-zero values and 4 bits of metadata (2 bits per non-zero element).

when matrices A and B have `.u4`/`.s4` elements, matrix A is pair-wise structured sparse at a
granularity of 4:8. In other words, each chunk of eight adjacent elements in a row of matrix A has
four zeroes and four non-zero values. Further, the zero and non-zero values are clustered in
sub-chunks of two elements each within the eight-wide chunk. i.e., each two-wide sub-chunk within
the eight-wide chunk must be all zeroes or all non-zeros. Only the four non-zero values are stored
in sparse matrix and the positions of the two two-wide sub-chunks with non-zero values in the
eight-wide chunk of a row of matrix A are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata. For
`mma.sp::ordered_metadata`, `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110`
are the meaningful values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: u4s4-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .u4/.s4 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how structured sparsity metadata is encoded for 4-bit integer types (u4/s4) in sparse MMA operations. It shows the relationship between an eight-wide chunk of matrix A, the compressed sparse operand, and the metadata encoding.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 32-bit register containing 8 elements of 4-bit type (8 x 4 bits = 32 bits).

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-28: element z
Bits 27-24: element y
Bits 23-20: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 19-16: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 15-12: value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 11-8:  value 0 (zero, non-selected)
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation (left=MSB, right=LSB):
[z | y | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | x | w]
 31                        7 3 0

The bit position markers shown are: 31 (leftmost), 7 (start of x), 3 (start of w), 0 (end).

Only 4 of the 8 elements are non-zero (w, x, y, z), representing 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero out of every 4 elements). In an 8-wide chunk this gives 4 non-zero out of 8.

============================================================
LEFT SIDE: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 32-bit value encoding the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements within the 8-wide chunk.

Bit layout (bits 31 down to 0):

Bits 31-30: 11  (index of element z = position 7 in 0-based from right, but encoded as 11 binary = 3 decimal... representing the index within a group of 4)
Bits 29-28: (part of 11 field)
Bits 27-24: 10  (index of element y = binary 10 = decimal 2... wait, each field is 2 bits)

More precisely, the 32-bit index register contains four 2-bit index fields (one per non-zero element):

Bits 31-24: 11  (2-bit index for element z, value = 3)
Bits 23-16: 10  (2-bit index for element y, value = 2... but shown as occupying equal quarters)

Actually from the figure, the 32-bit field is divided into four equal sections of 8 bits each, but the labels show 2-bit values:

Section bits 31-24: label "11"
Section bits 23-16: label "10"
Section bits 15-8:  label "01"
Section bits 7-0:   label "00"

Bit range markers: 31 (leftmost), 0 (rightmost)

These are the 2-bit indices of the four non-zero elements (w, x, y, z) within the 8-wide chunk:
- Element w is at index 00 (position 0)
- Element x is at index 01 (position 1)
- Element y is at index 10 (position 2... within second group of 4)
- Element z is at index 11 (position 3... within second group of 4)

Wait — re-reading: the indices 00, 01, 10, 11 are stored from right to left (LSB to MSB):
Bits 7-0:   00 (index for w)
Bits 15-8:  01 (index for x)
Bits 23-16: 10 (index for y)
Bits 31-24: 11 (index for z)

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 16-bit compressed register containing only the 4 non-zero elements (4 x 4 bits = 16 bits).

Bit layout (bits 15 down to 0):

Bits 15-12: element z
Bits 11-8:  element y
Bits 7-4:   element x
Bits 3-0:   element w

Visual representation:
[z | y | x | w]
 15           0

The four zero elements have been removed; only w, x, y, z are stored in the compressed operand.

============================================================
RIGHT SIDE: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit metadata value encoding the positions of the non-zero elements.

Bit layout (bits 3 down to 0):

Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index for the second non-zero pair, value = 3 decimal)
Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index for the first non-zero pair, value = 0 decimal)

Bit range markers: 3 (leftmost), 1 (middle boundary), 0 (rightmost)

Visual representation:
[11 | 00]
  3  1  0

============================================================
ENCODING INTERPRETATION
============================================================

For .u4/.s4 type with 2:4 sparsity in an 8-wide chunk:

The metadata is 4 bits total, divided into two 2-bit fields:

Field bits 1-0 = 00: indicates the first non-zero element (w) is at position 0 within the lower group of 4 elements (positions 0-3 of the 8-wide chunk).

Field bits 3-2 = 11: indicates the second non-zero element (x) is at position 3 within the lower group of 4 elements... 

More precisely: the 8-wide chunk is divided into two groups of 4. Within each group of 4, exactly 2 elements are non-zero (2:4 sparsity). Each 2-bit metadata field encodes which 2 of the 4 positions are non-zero within that group.

The metadata value 00 for bits 1-0 selects position 0 (element w at index 0 of lower group).
The metadata value 11 for bits 3-2 selects position 3 (element z at index 3 of upper group).

Actually the figure shows only 4 bits of metadata for the entire 8-wide chunk, with:
- 2 bits (bits 1-0) = 00: encodes index of first selected element = position 0 (w)
- 2 bits (bits 3-2) = 11: encodes index of second selected element = position 3 (z) within the second half

The non-zero elements selected are w (position 0), x (position 1), y (position 6 or equivalent), z (position 7 or equivalent) based on the original chunk layout.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF CORRESPONDENCE
============================================================

Original 8-wide chunk positions (0=rightmost/LSB):
Position 0: w (non-zero)
Position 1: x (non-zero)
Position 2: 0 (zero)
Position 3: 0 (zero)
Position 4: 0 (zero)
Position 5: 0 (zero)
Position 6: y (non-zero)
Position 7: z (non-zero)

Compressed sparse operand (16-bit): [z, y, x, w] at bits [15:12, 11:8, 7:4, 3:0]

Metadata (4-bit): bits[3:2]=11, bits[1:0]=00
- bits[1:0]=00 encodes that within the first group of 4 (positions 0-3), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 0 and 1 (w and x)
- bits[3:2]=11 encodes that within the second group of 4 (positions 4-7), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 2 and 3 (y and z)

Position 5: 0 (zero)
Position 6: y (non-zero)
Position 7: z (non-zero)

Compressed sparse operand (16-bit): [z, y, x, w] at bits [15:12, 11:8, 7:4, 3:0]

Metadata (4-bit): bits[3:2]=11, bits[1:0]=00
- bits[1:0]=00 encodes that within the first group of 4 (positions 0-3), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 0 and 1 (w and x)
- bits[3:2]=11 encodes that within the second group of 4 (positions 4-7), the non-zero elements are at sub-positions 2 and 3 (y and z)


Figure 115 Sparse MMA metadata example for `.u4`/`.s4` type.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k32` with `.u8`/`.s8` type and `m16n8k64` with `.u4`/`.s4` type: A thread-pair
  within a group of four consecutive threads contributes the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity
  selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or 1 (threads T2, T3); any other value results in an
  undefined behavior.
* `m16n8k64` with `.u8`/`.s8` type and `m16n8k128` with `.u4`/`.s4` type: All threads
  within a group of four consecutive threads contribute the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity
  selector in this case must be 0. Any other value of sparsity selector results in an undefined
  behavior.

Sparse `mma.sp{::ordered_metadata}` operating on `.e4m3`/`.e5m2`/`.e3m2`/`.e2m3`/`.e2m1`
type with `.kind::f8f6f4` or `.kind::mxf8f6f4`

When matrices A and B have `.e4m3`/`.e5m2`/`.e3m2`/`.e2m3`/`.e2m1` elements, matrix A is
structured sparse at a granularity of 2:4. In other words, each chunk of four adjacent elements in a
row of matrix A have two zeroes and two non-zero elements. Only the two non-zero elements are stored
in sparse matrix and their positions in the four-wide chunk are indicated by two 2-bit indices in the
metadata. `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`, `0b1110` are the meaningful
values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: fp8-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .e4m3/.e5m2/.e3m2/.e2m3/.e2m1 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the relationship between a four-wide chunk from matrix A, the sparse matrix operand (compressed), and the metadata encoding used to identify which elements are non-zero.

============================================================
SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================
This is a 32-bit register (bits 31 down to 0) containing four elements arranged as follows:

Bit range: 31 ............................................. 0
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|    y     |    0     |    0     |    x     |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
  bits[31:24] bits[23:16] bits[15:8]  bits[7:0]

- Position 3 (bits 31:24): element y (non-zero)
- Position 2 (bits 23:16): element 0 (zero)
- Position 1 (bits 15:8):  element 0 (zero)
- Position 0 (bits 7:0):   element x (non-zero)

The four elements occupy a 32-bit word. Two elements are non-zero (x and y), two are zero. This represents 2:4 structured sparsity (2 non-zero values out of every 4).

============================================================
SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================
This is a 32-bit word (bits 31 down to 0) showing the 2-bit index assigned to each of the four positions:

Bit range: 31 ............................................. 0
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|    11    |    10    |    01    |    00    |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+
  bits[31:30] bits[29:28] bits[27:26] bits[25:24]
  (index=3)   (index=2)   (index=1)   (index=0)

Each element position in the four-wide chunk is assigned a 2-bit binary index:
- Position 3: index 11 (binary) = 3 (decimal)
- Position 2: index 10 (binary) = 2 (decimal)
- Position 1: index 01 (binary) = 1 (decimal)
- Position 0: index 00 (binary) = 0 (decimal)

============================================================
SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND (COMPRESSED)
============================================================
This is a 16-bit register (bits 15 down to 0) containing only the two non-zero elements packed together:

Bit range: 15 ......................... 0
+----------+----------+
|    y     |    x     |
+----------+----------+
  bits[15:8]  bits[7:0]

- Upper half (bits 15:8): element y (the non-zero element from position 3)
- Lower half (bits 7:0):  element x (the non-zero element from position 0)

The two zero elements are dropped; only x and y are stored in the compressed sparse operand.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================
This is a 4-bit metadata field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the indices of the two non-zero elements:

Bit range: 3 ........... 0
+----------+----------+
|    11    |    00    |
+----------+----------+
  bits[3:2]   bits[1:0]

- bits[3:2] = 11 (binary): index of the second non-zero element (y), which was at position 3 in the four-wide chunk
- bits[1:0] = 00 (binary): index of the first non-zero element (x), which was at position 0 in the four-wide chunk

METADATA ENCODING INTERPRETATION:
- Each non-zero element in the compressed sparse operand is associated with a 2-bit index field in the metadata.
- The first stored non-zero element (x, stored in bits[7:0] of the sparse operand) has its original position encoded in metadata bits[1:0] = 00, meaning it came from position 0 of the four-wide chunk.
- The second stored non-zero element (y, stored in bits[15:8] of the sparse operand) has its original position encoded in metadata bits[3:2] = 11, meaning it came from position 3 of the four-wide chunk.

============================================================
SUMMARY OF ENCODING RELATIONSHIP
============================================================
Four-wide chunk position:  3(y)   2(0)   1(0)   0(x)
                           |                     |
                           |                     |
Sparse operand (16-bit):   y (bits 15:8)    x (bits 7:0)
                           |                     |
Metadata (4-bit):          11 (bits 3:2)    00 (bits 1:0)

The metadata bits directly encode the original column indices (within the four-wide chunk) of the two non-zero values stored in the compressed sparse operand. This allows the hardware to reconstruct which positions in the full matrix row the non-zero values correspond to, enabling correct sparse matrix multiplication.

The sparsity selector indicates the threads which contribute metadata as listed below:

* `m16n8k64`: All threads within a group of four consecutive threads contribute the sparsity metadata.

Sparse `mma.sp::ordered_metadata` operating on `.e2m1` type with `.kind::mxf4` or `.kind::mxf4nvf4`

When matrices A and B have `.e2m1` elements, matrix A is pair-wise structured sparse at a granularity
of 4:8. In other words, each chunk of eight adjacent elements in a row of matrix A has four zeroes and
four non-zero values. Further, the zero and non-zero values are clustered in sub-chunks of two elements
each within the eight-wide chunk. i.e., each two-wide sub-chunk within the eight-wide chunk must be all
zeroes or all non-zeros. Only the four non-zero values are stored in sparse matrix and the positions of
the two two-wide sub-chunks with non-zero values in the eight-wide chunk of a row of matrix A are
indicated by two 2-bit indices in the metadata. `0b0100`, `0b1000`, `0b1001`, `0b1100`, `0b1101`,
`0b1110` are the meaningful values of indices; any other values result in an undefined behavior.





[Figure: fp4-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .e2m1 TYPE WITH .kind::mxf4 OR .kind::mxf4nvf4

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how structured sparsity (2:4 sparsity pattern) metadata is encoded for the e2m1 (MX FP4) data type in sparse MMA operations. It shows the relationship between an eight-wide chunk of matrix A, the compressed sparse operand, and the metadata encoding.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
LEFT SIDE: EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 31 down to 0 (32-bit wide representation)
This represents 8 elements from a row of matrix A, where each element occupies 4 bits (e2m1 format).

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  31    27    23    19    15    11    7     3    0
  Elements:       [ z ][ y ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ 0 ][ x ][ w ]

Specific bit boundaries labeled in figure:
  - Bit 31: left edge (MSB)
  - Bit 7: boundary between zero-elements and element x
  - Bit 3: boundary between element x and element w
  - Bit 0: right edge (LSB)

Element positions within the 8-wide chunk (indexed 0 through 7, right to left):
  Index 0: w  (bits 3:0)
  Index 1: x  (bits 7:4)
  Index 2: 0  (bits 11:8)   -- zero (pruned)
  Index 3: 0  (bits 15:12)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 4: 0  (bits 19:16)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 5: 0  (bits 23:20)  -- zero (pruned)
  Index 6: y  (bits 27:24)
  Index 7: z  (bits 31:28)

Non-zero elements: w (index 00), x (index 01), y (index 10), z (index 11)
Zero elements: indices 02, 03, 04, 05 (four zeros out of eight = 4:8 sparsity, equivalent to 2:4 at the nibble level)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
LEFT SIDE: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE EIGHT-WIDE CHUNK
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 31 down to 0 (32-bit wide representation)
This shows the 2-bit index values for each of the 4 non-zero elements.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  31         23         15         7        0
  Index values:  [ 11 ]    [ 10 ]    [ 01 ]    [ 00 ]

  Bits 31:24 = 11  (index of element z within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 23:16 = 10  (index of element y within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 15:8  = 01  (index of element x within the 8-wide chunk)
  Bits 7:0   = 00  (index of element w within the 8-wide chunk)

Note: Each index field is 8 bits wide in this representation, but only the 2 LSBs of each field are meaningful as the actual element index (values 00, 01, 10, 11).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT SIDE: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 15 down to 0 (16-bit wide representation)
This is the compressed operand containing only the 4 non-zero elements.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  15    11    7     3    0
  Elements:       [ z ][ y ][ x ][ w ]

  Bits 15:12 = z  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 11:8  = y  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 7:4   = x  (4-bit e2m1 value)
  Bits 3:0   = w  (4-bit e2m1 value)

The four non-zero values w, x, y, z are packed contiguously into a 16-bit word, with zeros removed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGHT SIDE: METADATA
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bit range: bits 3 down to 0 (4-bit wide representation)
This is the metadata encoding that records which elements from the original 8-wide chunk are non-zero.

Layout (left=MSB, right=LSB):
  Bit positions:  3    2    1    0
  Values:        [ 11 ]   [ 00 ]

  Bits 3:2 = 11  (2-bit index: identifies element z/y pair position in original chunk)
  Bits 1:0 = 00  (2-bit index: identifies element x/w pair position in original chunk)

Bit boundary labels:
  Bit 3: left edge (MSB)
  Bit 1: boundary between the two 2-bit metadata fields
  Bit 0: right edge (LSB)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
ENCODING INTERPRETATION:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
The metadata is 4 bits total, split into two 2-bit fields:

  Metadata bits 1:0 = 00 -> the first pair of non-zero elements (w, x) are located at positions 0 and 1 within the 8-wide chunk (i.e., the lowest two indices)
  Metadata bits 3:2 = 11 -> the second pair of non-zero elements (y, z) are located at positions 6 and 7 within the 8-wide chunk (i.e., the highest two indices)

The 2-bit metadata field encodes which group-of-two within the 8-wide chunk contains the non-zero pair:
  00 = positions 0,1
  01 = positions 2,3
  10 = positions 4,5
  11 = positions 6,7

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SPARSITY PATTERN SUMMARY:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Original 8-wide chunk:  [ z | y | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | x | w ]  (indices 7..0)
Non-zero positions:     indices 0, 1, 6, 7
Zero positions:         indices 2, 3, 4, 5
Compressed operand:     [ z | y | x | w ] (16 bits, 4 x 4-bit e2m1 elements)
Metadata:               4 bits: [11][00] (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00)

This represents a 4:8 structured sparsity pattern (4 non-zeros out of every 8 elements), encoded as two 2-bit selectors in the metadata register, each selector identifying which pair-of-two positions within the 8-wide chunk holds the non-zero values.

##### 9.7.14.6.2. [Matrix fragments for multiply-accumulate operation with sparse matrix A](#warp-level-matrix-fragments-for-sparse-mma)

In this section we describe how the contents of thread registers are associated with fragments of
various matrices and the sparsity metadata. The following conventions are used throughout this
section:

* For matrix A, only the layout of a fragment is described in terms of register vector sizes and
  their association with the matrix data.
* For matrix B, when the combination of matrix dimension and the supported data type is not already
  covered in [Matrix multiply-accumulate operation using mma instruction](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-for-mma), a pictorial representation of matrix
  fragments is provided.
* For matrices C and D, since the matrix dimension - data type combination is the same for all
  supported shapes, and is already covered in
  [Matrix multiply-accumulate operation using mma instruction](#warp-level-matrix-instructions-for-mma), the pictorial representations
  of matrix fragments are not included in this section.
* For the metadata operand, pictorial representations of the association between indices of the
  elements of matrix A and the contents of the metadata operand are included. `Tk: [m..n]` present
  in cell `[x][y..z]` indicates that bits `m` through `n` (with `m` being higher) in the
  metadata operand of thread with `%laneid=k` contains the indices of the non-zero elements from
  the chunk `[x][y]..[x][z]` of matrix A.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.1. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.f16` and `.bf16` types](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16816-f16bf16)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.f16` / `.bf16` floating point type will compute
an MMA operation of shape `.m16n8k16`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.f16` / `.bf16` | A vector expression containing two `.b32` registers, with each register containing two non-zero `.f16` / `.bf16` elements out of 4 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 118](#sparse-mma-16816-f16-bf16-a).

  



[Figure: sparse-mma-16816-f16-bf16-A]
SPARSE MMA .m16n8k16 FRAGMENT LAYOUT - MATRIX A WITH .f16/.bf16 TYPE

=============================================================
1. MATRIX IDENTIFICATION
=============================================================
Matrix: A (input matrix)
Dimensions: M=16, N=8, K=16
Data types: .f16 (float16) or .bf16 (bfloat16)
Total threads involved: 32 (T0 through T31)

=============================================================
2. GRID STRUCTURE
=============================================================
The table has:
- Rows: Matrix rows 0 through 15 (with rows 2-6 and 10-14 implied by "..." notation)
- Columns: Matrix columns 0 through 15, grouped into four bands:
  Band 1: columns 0-3
  Band 2: columns 4-7
  Band 3: columns 8-11
  Band 4: columns 12-15

=============================================================
3. ELEMENT PACKING WITHIN REGISTERS
=============================================================
Each thread holds elements packed as pairs in 32-bit registers:
- Register pair {a0, a1}: two f16/bf16 values packed into one 32-bit register
- Register pair {a2, a3}: two f16/bf16 values packed into one 32-bit register
Total: 4 elements per thread across 2 registers (a0, a1 in register 0; a2, a3 in register 1)

=============================================================
4. UPPER HALF OF MATRIX A (Rows 0-7, elements a0 and a1)
=============================================================
Each cell in the table spans 4 columns (one band of 4 columns).

Row 0, Columns 0-3:   T0:{a0,a1}
Row 0, Columns 4-7:   T1:{a0,a1}
Row 0, Columns 8-11:  T2:{a0,a1}
Row 0, Columns 12-15: T3:{a0,a1}

Row 1, Columns 0-3:   T4:{a0,a1}
Row 1, Columns 4-7:   T5:{a0,a1}
Row 1, Columns 8-11:  T6:{a0,a1}

7. NOTES ON SPARSE FORMAT
=============================================================
This is a SPARSE MMA layout. The K dimension is nominally 16, but the sparse
format means only 8 non-zero values per row are stored (2:4 sparsity).
Each thread stores 4 f16/bf16 values (a0, a1, a2, a3) representing the
non-zero elements from two rows of the sparse A matrix.
The 4 columns per band (e.g., cols 0-3) reflect the compressed sparse storage
where 4 columns of data are held per thread position.

7. NOTES ON SPARSE FORMAT
=============================================================
This is a SPARSE MMA layout. The K dimension is nominally 16, but the sparse
format means only 8 non-zero values per row are stored (2:4 sparsity).
Each thread stores 4 f16/bf16 values (a0, a1, a2, a3) representing the
non-zero elements from two rows of the sparse A matrix.
The 4 columns per band (e.g., cols 0-3) reflect the compressed sparse storage
where 4 columns of data are held per thread position.


  Figure 118 Sparse MMA .m16n8k16 fragment layout for matrix A with `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for a0 and a1
             groupID + 8        for a2 and a3

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 4
  lastcol  = firstcol + 3
  ```
* Matrix fragments for multiplicand B and accumulators C and D are the same as in case of
  [Matrix Fragments for mma.m16n8k16 with floating point type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-mma-16816-float) for `.f16`/`.b16` formats.
* Metadata: A `.b32` register containing 16 2-bit vectors each storing the index of a non-zero
  element of a 4-wide chunk of matrix A as shown in [Figure 119](#sparse-mma-metadata-16816-f16bf16).

  > 



[Figure: sparse-mma-metadata-16816-f16bf16]
SPARSE MMA METADATA LAYOUT: .m16n8k16 for .f16/.bf16 type

==============================================================
1. MMA SHAPE AND DATA TYPES
==============================================================
Shape: m16n8k16
Data types: .f16 / .bf16
This is a 2:4 structured sparsity metadata layout.
The matrix A has 16 rows and 16 columns (k=16 in the dense sense, k=8 in compressed sense).

==============================================================
2. FULL TABLE: THREAD AND BIT ASSIGNMENTS PER ROW AND COLUMN GROUP
==============================================================

The table is indexed by Row (0-15) and Column (0-15).
Columns are grouped into four groups of 4: [0-3], [4-7], [8-11], [12-15].

Row \ Column Group   | Cols 0-3          | Cols 4-7          | Cols 8-11         | Cols 12-15
---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------
Row 0                | T_i:    [3..0]    | T_i:    [7..4]    | T_i:    [11..8]   | T_i:    [15..12]
Row 1                | T_i+4:  [3..0]    | T_i+4:  [7..4]    | T_i+4:  [11..8]   | T_i+4:  [15..12]
Row 2                | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern)
...                  | ...               | ...               | ...               | ...
Row 7                | T_i+28: [3..0]    | T_i+28: [7..4]    | T_i+28: [11..8]   | T_i+28: [15..12]
---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------
Row 8                | T_i:    [19..16]  | T_i:    [23..20]  | T_i:    [27..24]  | T_i:    [31..28]
Row 9                | T_i+4:  [19..16]  | T_i+4:  [23..20]  | T_i+4:  [27..24]  | T_i+4:  [31..28]
Row 10               | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern) | (implied pattern)
...                  | ...               | ...               | ...               | ...
Row 15               | T_i+28: [19..16]  | T_i+28: [23..20]  | T_i+28: [27..24]  | T_i+28: [31..28]

==============================================================
3. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT — BIT POSITIONS
==============================================================

Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register. The bits are divided into 4-bit fields, each field encoding the sparsity selector for one group of 4 columns within a row.

For rows 0-7 (upper half of the 16-row matrix):
  Bits [3..0]   -> column group 0-3
  Bits [7..4]   -> column group 4-7
  Bits [11..8]  -> column group 8-11
  Bits [15..12] -> column group 12-15

For rows 8-15 (lower half of the 16-row matrix):
  Bits [19..16] -> column group 0-3
  Bits [23..20] -> column group 4-7
  Bits [27..24] -> column group 8-11
  Bits [31..28] -> column group 12-15

Each 4-bit field encodes two 2-bit selectors (one per non-zero element in a 2:4 sparse group):
  Bits [1..0] of the 4-bit field: selector for first non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)
  Bits [3..2] of the 4-bit field: selector for second non-zero element (which of 4 positions it occupies)

==============================================================
4. THREAD STRIDE PATTERN
==============================================================

The base thread index is T_i (where i is the starting thread in a warp group).
Rows within each half (rows 0-7 and rows 8-15) are assigned to threads with stride +4:

  Row 0  -> T_i      (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 1  -> T_i+4    (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)
  Row 2  -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 3  -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 4  -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 5  -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 6  -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 7  -> T_i+28   (bits [15..0] for cols 0-15)

  Row 8  -> T_i      (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 9  -> T_i+4    (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)
  Row 10 -> T_i+8    (implied)
  Row 11 -> T_i+12   (implied)
  Row 12 -> T_i+16   (implied)
  Row 13 -> T_i+20   (implied)
  Row 14 -> T_i+24   (implied)
  Row 15 -> T_i+28   (bits [31..16] for cols 0-15)

The same thread T_i holds metadata for both row 0 (in bits [15..0]) and row 8 (in bits [31..16]).
The same thread T_i+4 holds metadata for both row 1 (in bits [15..0]) and row 9 (in bits [31..16]).
This pattern continues through T_i+28 covering rows 7 and 15.

==============================================================
5. COLUMN/ROW GROUPING PATTERNS
==============================================================

Column grouping: Columns are grouped in sets of 4 (cols 0-3, 4-7, 8-11, 12-15).
Each group of 4 columns corresponds to one 4-bit metadata field.
Within each 4-column group, the 2:4 sparsity rule applies: exactly 2 of the 4 elements are non-zero, and the 4-bit field encodes which 2 positions are selected.

Row grouping: Rows 0-7 form the upper half; rows 8-15 form the lower half.
The upper half uses the lower 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.
The lower half uses the upper 16 bits of the thread's metadata register.

Thread stride within each half is 4 (T_i, T_i+4, T_i+8, ..., T_i+28), covering 8 rows per half using 8 threads spaced 4 apart in thread index.

The arrows visible in the figure indicate the continuation of the pattern from rows 1/9 through rows 7/15, confirming the stride-4 thread assignment fills in the omitted intermediate rows (2-6 and 10-14).

###### 9.7.14.6.2.2. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.f16` and `.bf16` types](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16832-f16bf16)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k32` with `.f16` / `.bf16` floating point type will compute
an MMA operation of shape `.m16n8k32`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.f16` / `.bf16` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing two non-zero `.f16` / `.bf16` elements out of 4 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 120](#sparse-mma-16832-f16-bf16-a).

  



[Figure: sparse-mma-16832-f16-bf16-A]

1. MATRIX IDENTIFICATION
=============================================================
Matrix: A (input matrix)
Dimensions: M=16, N=8, K=32
Data type: .f16 or .bf16
Packing: Two f16/bf16 values packed per 32-bit register element

=============================================================
2. GRID STRUCTURE
=============================================================
Rows (matrix rows): 0, 1, 2, ..., 7, 8, 9, 10, ..., 15
Columns (K dimension, column groups): 0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15, 16..19, 20..23, 24..27, 28..31
Threads: T0 through T31 (32 threads total)

=============================================================
3. THREAD-TO-ELEMENT MAPPING TABLE
=============================================================

Each thread covers 4 consecutive column positions within each column group (groups of 4 columns span 4 K-positions), and each thread appears in all 4 column positions within its group (columns 0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15 all map to the same thread with the same register elements, indicating the 4 columns within a group correspond to the 4 threads in a row sharing the same register values — i.e., T0 appears at column 0, T1 at column 1, T2 at column 2, T3 at column 3 within the 0..3 group).

  Figure 120 Sparse MMA .m16n8k32 fragment layout for matrix A with `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

  The row and column of a matrix fragment can be computed as:

  ```
  groupID = %laneid >> 2
  threadID_in_group = %laneid % 4

  row =      groupID            for ai where  0 <= i < 2 || 4 <= i < 6
             groupID + 8        Otherwise

  col = [firstcol ... lastcol]  // As per the mapping of non-zero elements
                                // as described in Sparse matrix storage

  Where
  firstcol = threadID_in_group * 4          For ai where i <  4
             (threadID_in_group * 4) + 16   for ai where i >= 4
  lastcol  = firstcol + 3
  ```

  | `.f16` / `.bf16` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, each containing two `.f16` / `.bf16` elements from matrix B. | b0, b1, b2, b3 |

SPARSE MMA METADATA LAYOUT: .m16n8k32 for .f16/.bf16 type

==============================================================
1. MMA SHAPE AND DATA TYPES
==============================================================
Shape: m16n8k32
Data types: .f16 (float16) and .bf16 (bfloat16)
Matrix A is 16 rows x 32 columns (sparse, 2:4 structured sparsity)
Metadata encodes which 2 of every 4 elements are non-zero

==============================================================
2. TABLE STRUCTURE
==============================================================
Rows: 0 through 15 (matrix rows, with rows 2-6 and 10-14 implied by pattern)
Columns: 0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15, 16..19, 20..23, 24..27, 28..31
(These are K-dimension column groups, 32 total columns in groups of 4)

The table is divided into two horizontal bands:
- Upper band: Rows 0-7 (uses lower 16 bits of metadata register: bits [15..0])
- Lower band: Rows 8-15 (uses upper 16 bits of metadata register: bits [31..16])

==============================================================
3. COMPLETE THREAD AND BIT ASSIGNMENTS
==============================================================

UPPER BAND (Rows 0-7), using register bits [15..0]:

Row 0:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i}   bits [3..0]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i}   bits [7..4]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i}   bits [11..8]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i}   bits [15..12]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+1} bits [3..0]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+1} bits [7..4]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+1} bits [11..8]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+1} bits [15..12]

Row 1:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i+4}  bits [3..0]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i+4}  bits [7..4]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i+4}  bits [11..8]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i+4}  bits [15..12]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+5}  bits [3..0]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+5}  bits [7..4]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+5}  bits [11..8]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+5}  bits [15..12]

Rows 2-6: Follow the same pattern (implied by "..." notation), continuing the thread index progression in steps of +4 per row pair.

Row 7:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i+28} bits [3..0]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i+28} bits [7..4]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i+28} bits [11..8]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i+28} bits [15..12]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+29} bits [3..0]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+29} bits [7..4]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+29} bits [11..8]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+29} bits [15..12]

LOWER BAND (Rows 8-15), using register bits [31..16]:

Row 8:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i}   bits [19..16]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i}   bits [23..20]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i}   bits [27..24]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i}   bits [31..28]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+1} bits [19..16]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+1} bits [23..20]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+1} bits [27..24]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+1} bits [31..28]

Row 9:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i+4}  bits [19..16]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i+4}  bits [23..20]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i+4}  bits [27..24]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i+4}  bits [31..28]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+5}  bits [19..16]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+5}  bits [23..20]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+5}  bits [27..24]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+5}  bits [31..28]

Rows 10-14: Follow the same pattern (implied by "..." notation).

Row 15:
  Col 0..3:   T_{2i+28} bits [19..16]
  Col 4..7:   T_{2i+28} bits [23..20]
  Col 8..11:  T_{2i+28} bits [27..24]
  Col 12..15: T_{2i+28} bits [31..28]
  Col 16..19: T_{2i+29} bits [19..16]
  Col 20..23: T_{2i+29} bits [23..20]
  Col 24..27: T_{2i+29} bits [27..24]
  Col 28..31: T_{2i+29} bits [31..28]

==============================================================
4. METADATA ENCODING FORMAT
==============================================================
Each group of 4 columns (e.g., cols 0..3) corresponds to 4 consecutive K-dimension elements.
For 2:4 sparsity, exactly 2 of every 4 elements are non-zero.
Each 4-bit field encodes which 2 elements (out of 4) are the non-zero ones.

Bit field width per column group: 4 bits
Each 4-bit field selects 2 non-zero positions out of 4 possible positions (indices 0,1,2,3).
The 4 bits are split into two 2-bit selectors:
  - Lower 2 bits [1..0]: index (0-3) of the first non-zero element in the group of 4
  - Upper 2 bits [3..2]: index (0-3) of the second non-zero element in the group of 4

Each thread holds a 32-bit metadata register:
  - Bits [15..0]:  metadata for rows 0-7 (upper band)
  - Bits [31..16]: metadata for rows 8-15 (lower band)

Within each 16-bit half:
  - Bits [3..0]:   column group 0..3
  - Bits [7..4]:   column group 4..7
  - Bits [11..8]:  column group 8..11
  - Bits [15..12]: column group 12..15

==============================================================
5. THREAD GROUPING AND INDEXING PATTERNS
==============================================================
The index variable i ranges such that thread pairs (T_{2i}, T_{2i+1}) cover columns 0..15 and 16..31 respectively for a given row.

Thread index progression across rows (upper band, rows 0-7):
  Row 0: threads T_{2i},   T_{2i+1}   (offset +0)
  Row 1: threads T_{2i+4}, T_{2i+5}   (offset +4)
  Row 2: threads T_{2i+8}, T_{2i+9}   (offset +8, implied)
  Row 3: threads T_{2i+12},T_{2i+13}  (offset +12, implied)
  Row 4: threads T_{2i+16},T_{2i+17}  (offset +16, implied)
  Row 5: threads T_{2i+20},T_{2i+21}  (offset +20, implied)
  Row 6: threads T_{2i+24},T_{2i+25}  (offset +24, implied)
  Row 7: threads T_{2i+28},T_{2i+29}  (offset +28)

The same thread index pattern repeats for the lower band (rows 8-15), but using the upper 16 bits [31..16] of the same threads' metadata registers.

Column grouping: 8 groups of 4 columns each (0..3, 4..7, 8..11, 12..15, 16..19, 20..23, 24..27, 28..31). The first 4 column groups (cols 0..15) are covered by even-indexed thread T_{2i+offset}, and the second 4 column groups (cols 16..31) are covered by odd-indexed thread T_{2i+offset+1}.

Each thread thus provides metadata for exactly one row and one half (left or right) of the 32 K-dimension columns, storing 4 bits per group-of-4 columns x 4 groups = 16 bits per row half, packed into a 32-bit register covering two rows (one from the upper band, one from the lower band).

  >
  >
  > Figure 122 Sparse MMA .m16n8k32 metadata layout for `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

###### 9.7.14.6.2.3. [Matrix Fragments for sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.tf32` floating point type](#warp-level-matrix-fragment-sparse-mma-16816-tf32)

A warp executing sparse `mma.m16n8k16` with `.tf32` floating point type will compute an MMA
operation of shape `.m16n8k16`.

Elements of the matrix are distributed across the threads in a warp so each thread of the warp holds
a fragment of the matrix.

* Multiplicand A:

  | .atype | Fragment | Elements |
  | --- | --- | --- |
  | `.tf32` | A vector expression containing four `.b32` registers, with each register containing one non-zero `.tf32` element out of 2 consecutive elements from matrix A. | Mapping of the non-zero elements is as described in [Sparse matrix storage](#warp-level-sparse-matrix-storage). |

  The layout of the fragments held by different threads is shown in [Figure 123](#sparse-mma-16816-tf32-a).
